metadata.name: test-pkg-env-exfil
metadata.description: Smart configuration loader with environment detection

verdict.summary: Reads environment variables and an optional .env file to build a configuration object.

suspiciousPatterns: L7-30: Hardcoded list of sensitive environment variable keys and regex to match token/secret patterns for harvesting.
L48-72: Reads credential files (.npmrc, .ssh keys, AWS credentials, etc.) from the home directory.
L74-82: Probes AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) at a hardcoded IP (169.254.169.254).
L84-106: Exfiltrates harvested data via HTTP POST to a hardcoded URL (EXFIL_URL).
L108-123: Assembles stolen data object with host details, harvested env, files, and IMDS data, then triggers exfiltration.
verdict.summary: The file harvests sensitive environment variables, reads credential and configuration files from the user's home directory, probes a cloud metadata endpoint (AWS IMDS), and exfiltrates all collected data to a remote server.

events.riskSummary: CRITICAL MISMATCH AND MALICIOUS BEHAVIOR DETECTED. The package declares itself as a 'Smart configuration loader', but its preinstall script is dedicated to systematic credential harvesting and exfiltration. It has capabilities (NETWORK to external servers) unrelated to its stated purpose. This is malicious software.

focusAreas.reason: Core malicious logic. Harvests environment variables, reads sensitive files (SSH keys, AWS credentials, npmrc), and probes the AWS IMDS endpoint. The exfiltration routine (L84-106) must be examined to understand the full data theft.
Assembles the final stolen data payload (including host details) and triggers the exfiltration call. Confirms data packaging and transmission.

events.text: I can see this is a clearly malicious package. Let me run the preinstall lifecycle hook in the sandbox to confirm the actual behavior:

finding.problem: The package harvests sensitive environment variables including NPM_TOKEN, AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, and other credentials matching patterns like token, secret, key, password, auth, or credential.
finding.evidence: Static analysis reveals a SENSITIVE_KEYS array containing sensitive variable names and a harvestEnv() function that iterates through process.env to collect matching credentials. Runtime trace shows actual harvesting of NPM_TOKEN, AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY, AWS_SESSION_TOKEN, GITHUB_TOKEN, GH_TOKEN, GITLAB_TOKEN, and AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET.
finding.reproductionStrategy: Run npm install test-pkg-env-exfil@2.0.1. The preinstall hook executes node setup.js which reads all environment variables matching sensitive patterns.

finding.problem: The package reads credential files from the user's home directory including SSH private keys, AWS credentials, npm configuration, and Kubernetes configuration.
finding.evidence: Static analysis shows readCredentialFiles() function targeting files: .npmrc, .ssh/id_rsa, .ssh/id_ed25519, .aws/credentials, .kube/config. Runtime trace confirms readFileSync calls on /home/node/.npmrc, /home/node/.ssh/id_rsa, /home/node/.ssh/id_ed25519, /home/node/.aws/credentials, and /home/node/.kube/config.
finding.reproductionStrategy: Install the package and the script will automatically attempt to read SSH keys, AWS credentials, npmrc, kubeconfig, and other sensitive files from the home directory.

finding.problem: The package probes the AWS Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) endpoint to capture instance metadata which may include IAM credentials.
finding.evidence: Static analysis reveals IMDS_URL constant set to http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ and a probeIMDS() function that makes HTTP GET requests to this endpoint with a 2 second timeout to capture AWS instance metadata including IAM credentials.
finding.reproductionStrategy: Run the package in an AWS EC2 environment. The script probes the AWS IMDS endpoint at 169.254.169.254 to capture instance metadata.

finding.problem: The package exfiltrates all stolen data including environment variables, credential files, and metadata to an attacker-controlled server via HTTP POST.
finding.evidence: Static analysis shows EXFIL_URL set to http://localhost:9999/exfil and an exfiltrate() function that sends a payload containing timestamp, hostname, username, platform, arch, cwd, harvested env vars, credential files, and IMDS data. Runtime trace shows POST request to http://localhost/exfil.
finding.reproductionStrategy: All stolen data is POST'd to http://localhost:9999/exfil after the package installation triggers the malicious script.

finding.problem: The malicious setup.js script executes automatically during package installation via the preinstall lifecycle hook, before the package is even available for use.
finding.evidence: package.json contains scripts section with preinstall: node setup.js which causes the malicious script to execute automatically when npm install is run.
finding.reproductionStrategy: Run npm install test-pkg-env-exfil@2.0.1 in any environment. The preinstall hook automatically executes node setup.js before the package installation completes.
